does: This paper identifies and quantifies descriptive collision in sparse autoencoders—the problem where many distinct SAE features receive the same natural-language explanation despite representing different learned concepts. Using the largest publicly available dataset of human-annotated SAE features from Marks et al. (2025), comprising 722 annotations across Gemma 2 2B and Pythia 70M, the authors demonstrate that the mean annotation string is reused across 3.07 features, with 82.1% of features sharing annotations with at least one other feature. They show that the single most common annotation ("plural nouns") describes 101 distinct features across 18 layers and four model components. Through information-theoretic analysis, they establish that the average annotation resolves only 70% of feature identity, revealing a fundamental limit in current automated interpretability approaches that assign brief explanations to SAE features.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on mechanistic interpretability and feature visualization in neural networks, directly extending the sparse autoencoder framework developed by Sharkey et al. and others as a tool for decomposing model activations into interpretable components. It responds to and complements existing critiques of automated interpretability, particularly work addressing polysemanticity (single features with multiple meanings) such as that by Elhage et al. and Cunningham et al. The paper engages with the reanalysis of human-annotated features from Marks et al. (2025), building on systematic efforts to validate whether automatically-discovered features correspond to human-understandable concepts and whether explanations reliably predict feature activations.
enables: This work establishes both a fundamental problem and a framework for future research in automated interpretability. It motivates the development of richer explanation schemes that can distinguish between features admitted by the same simple descriptions, potentially through structured or multi-part annotations. The information-theoretic formalization suggests paths toward quantifying explanation quality beyond activation prediction, enabling better evaluation metrics for interpretability tools. The findings point toward hybrid human-AI annotation systems that can iteratively refine explanations when collisions are detected, and toward model architectural changes or SAE training procedures designed to reduce feature redundancy. This research also highlights the need for interpretability methods operating at finer granularity than current brief natural-language annotations can provide.
